findings.description: Expiry check is inverted: returns age < ttl, so fresh entries are treated as expired and stale entries are served indefinitely.
Because _is_expired is inverted, valid fresh L1 entries are deleted on line 54 while expired ones fall through to be returned as hits.
pickle.loads on bytes read from a shared/network Redis store is untrusted deserialization, enabling arbitrary code execution if an attacker can write the key.
After an L1 entry is judged expired and deleted, the code falls through to Redis and reloads/serves the same value, so L1 TTL is never truly enforced against L2.
_local has no max-size bound or eviction policy, so it grows without limit (only broken expiry-on-access trims it), causing unbounded memory growth.
Cache is not thread-safe: _local dict mutations (assignment on 61/68, del on 54, pop on 81) are unsynchronized despite a shared tiered cache being typically accessed concurrently.
setex ignores ttl_seconds, so the L2 store never expires and the cache's TTL contract is silently broken at the Redis tier.
CacheStats.local_entries is updated only in set() but not when get() populates _local on line 61, so the tracked counter drifts from the real cache size.
Both L1 and L2 reads increment a single hits counter with no L1-vs-L2 distinction, so hit-rate metrics cannot reveal L1 effectiveness.
No error handling around pickle.loads or json operations: a corrupt/poison Redis value raises an unhandled UnpicklingError that propagates to every caller of get().
Module-level logger is configured but never used, leaving cache faults and evictions with no observability.
